justification: All five rubric criteria for pass are met: (1) Audit log gap clearly identified — 'Admin action performed without the operation or target user makes forensic investigation impossible', with specific callouts for missing operation, target_user_id, parameters, and outcome. (2) PII-in-logs risk identified — customer name, address, card last 4 flagged as data leak vector and PCI-DSS/GDPR violation. (3) Stack trace in 500 responses identified as information disclosure — 'Full Java stack traces in 500 responses expose class names, library versions, file paths — direct aid to attackers.' (4) Rate limiting absence on public-facing admin API identified as brute-force/credential stuffing risk. (5) Public internet exposure escalated appropriately — routed to 'Coach K → Security Team' rather than unilaterally mandated, and RBAC escalated to 'Coach K → Bird + Business Stakeholders'. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Excellent escalation discipline — RBAC decision routed to Bird/business stakeholders, network restriction routed to security team, PII compliance routed to legal. Pippen correctly identifies these as beyond his authority.
Audit log gap analysis is exceptionally detailed — identifies not just that operation is missing but also target_user_id, parameters, outcome, and correlation_id.
PII-in-logs correctly tied to specific compliance frameworks (PCI-DSS, GDPR) rather than vague 'security concern'.
Stack trace risk correctly contextualized with OWASP reference and specific details exposed (class names, library versions, file paths).
Stub /health endpoint identified as anti-pattern — load balancer routing to broken instances.
Strong operational perspective throughout — the '3am on-call' scenario effectively illustrates why audit log gaps matter in practice.

Minor: RBAC is listed as a MUST-HAVE ship-blocker (#2) which borders on mandating rather than escalating, though the escalations section correctly routes it to business stakeholders. Slight tension between the two sections.
